author: jih2nn
content: I was assuming anomaly situations such as 51% attack, client bugs, bad network, etc. It's not likely but we should be prepared IMO. Maybe I'm overcomplicating it 😅 I need to think more about this. Also, please note that `A` is IL-compliant! It was a typo.

author: nflaig
content:
> Why can't we modify get_head such that any any block extending from non IL compliant payload will not be considered as head block?
I am in the same camp as Jihoon, will need to think more about it, but I don't really see how you can do that without putting ILs into the block.

author: jih2nn
content:
Ah, I missed this line.

> Why can't we modify get_head such that any any block extending from non IL compliant payload will not be considered as head block?

That makes IL enforcement permanent and we shouldn't do that. Imagine a case that we have split view with the following conditions:

- All active validators are honest.
- A set of 16 ILs is partitioned into two disjoint subsets `IL_A` and `IL_B`.
- Half of the validators received `IL_A` and the other half received `IL_B`, with the attesters evenly split between the two groups.
- The proposer received `IL_A`.
- The payload satisfies `IL_A` but not `IL_B`.

With the persistent enforcement, this will split the chain forever even without any malicious validators. This might happen when the p2p network becomes unreliable for whatever reason such as client bugs, cloud provider outages, BGP hijacks, etc.

author: jih2nn
content: Looks good, thanks! I've pushed a fix to the failing test. Let me merge the PR once CI is green.
